planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed. In zone 9 start in January–February and transplant February–March. Provide sturdy cage or stake support for the large, indeterminate vines. The bicolor flesh — yellow-gold base with red internal streaking — makes Gold Medal one of the most beautiful tomatoes when sliced. In zone 9 fall planting in August produces exceptional flavor.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil with sturdy stake or cage support; Gold Medal (also known as B&L's Gold Medal) is a large, beefsteak-type heirloom tomato producing spectacular bicolor fruits with bright golden-yellow skin blushed with red and dramatically marbled interior flesh — when sliced, the cross-section reveals a swirled pattern of deep red-orange and golden-yellow that makes Gold Medal arguably the most beautiful heirloom tomato variety for fresh-cut presentations and tomato salads. The flavor combines the mild, low-acid sweetness characteristic of yellow tomatoes with the rich, complex notes of a large beefsteak type, producing a fruit that is simultaneously sweet and substantial in flavor without the sharp acidity of most large red heirlooms. The low-acid, sweet character makes Gold Medal appealing for people who enjoy the fresh sweetness of yellow tomatoes but want a larger, meatier fruit. Large fruits commonly reach 1–2 pounds and have meaty walls with small seed cavities, making them outstanding for fresh slicing.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Gold Medal Heirloom Tomato
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Gold Medal Bicolor Tomato
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Bicolor Tomato Varieties

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 70°F; in zone 9 plant March–May. Canary Melon is ripe when the skin turns from yellow-green to vivid canary yellow and the fruit gives slightly under thumb pressure at the blossom end. Unlike netted muskmelons, Canary does not separate from the vine easily when ripe — check for color, firmness, and fragrance rather than slip. The thick rind allows extended storage (several weeks at room temperature) compared to most melons.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Canary Melon (also called Juan Canary or amarillo melon) is an oblong to football-shaped melon with dramatically vivid, glossy, bright canary-yellow skin that makes it one of the most visually striking melons in the market or garden — the name describes the intense yellow of a canary bird's plumage. The creamy white to very pale green flesh is smooth, firm-crisp rather than soft, mildly sweet, and extremely mild-flavored with a delicate freshness completely different from the musky richness of cantaloupe — the flavor is subtle and refreshing, with a light sweetness and almost no aroma. This delicate flavor profile is characteristic of Spanish-type melons (inodorus group), which are also notable for their thick rind and long post-harvest shelf life. Canary Melon is widely grown in Spain and is a standard melon type in Spanish and Latin American markets. In zone 9 the long, warm summer produces the full sweetness that Canary Melon develops best in extended heat.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Specialty Melon Varieties
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Watermelon and Melon Production
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Specialty Melons for Gulf South

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 70°F; in zone 9 plant March–May. Carnival is a compact acorn-type squash on a semi-bush vine well suited to smaller gardens. No two fruits look identical — the cream, orange, and green patterning varies on every squash, making Carnival ornamentally striking for fall displays as well as edible. Cure harvested fruits for 2 weeks at room temperature before eating to improve sweetness; storage improves flavor.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Carnival is an All-America Selections award-winning acorn-type winter squash that produces medium-sized (1–2 lb), acorn-shaped fruits in an extraordinarily variable pattern of cream, orange, yellow, and green — no two Carnival squash are identical in coloration, with each fruit displaying a unique combination of the variety's characteristic colors in an irregular patterned display. The diversity of color on each individual squash and across the harvest makes Carnival as ornamental as it is edible, used for autumn fall displays, Thanksgiving centerpieces, and decorative arrangements alongside pumpkins. The flesh is orange, mildly sweet, and nutty — essentially the same eating quality as high-quality acorn squash, with a dry, dense texture that accepts butter, brown sugar, or maple syrup preparations well. Carnival is extremely productive and compact for an acorn-type, making it practical for backyard gardens with limited space.
sources: Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Winter Squash Variety Trial
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Winter Squash Production

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Hutterite Soup Bean is grown primarily as a dry bean — allow pods to dry fully on the vine before threshing. In zone 9 the summer heat accelerates pod drying; harvest entire plants when most pods are dry and thresh indoors out of weather. Beans dry best in the pod; shell after the pods are fully crisp to avoid shattering. Store dried beans in cool, dark, dry conditions for multi-year storage.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil; Hutterite Soup Bean is a pole bean heirloom preserved by the Hutterite communities of the northern Great Plains and Canada — religious agricultural communities known for maintaining exceptional heirloom seed collections. The small, round, pale yellow seeds are outstanding soup beans with a creamy, slightly buttery, mild flavor and a texture that breaks down smoothly in long-simmered soups without becoming mushy — they were specifically selected and maintained by Hutterite cooks for soup making over many generations. The small seed size (similar to a navy bean) and mild flavor make Hutterite Soup Bean versatile in white bean soups, bean salads, and cassoulet-style dishes. The climbing pole bean vines are productive and the pods dry down cleanly for harvest. The Hutterite selection demonstrates exceptional adaptation to cool, short-season climates of the northern plains, but grows successfully in a wide range of conditions.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Hutterite Soup Bean Heirloom
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Hutterite Soup Bean
Montana State University Extension — Heirloom Dry Bean Varieties

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed. In zone 9 start in January–February and transplant March–April. Black Pearl is compact (18–24 inches) and extremely ornamental — it is widely grown as a container plant where the black foliage and shiny black fruits transitioning to red create continuous seasonal interest. In zone 9 the plant can overwinter as a perennial woody shrub, regrowth from the base producing even heavier crops in subsequent seasons.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Black Pearl is an All-America Selections award-winning ornamental pepper notable for its intensely dark, shiny, almost black foliage throughout the growing season — one of the darkest-leaved edible plants available for ornamental garden use. The small, round, pea-sized fruits emerge shiny black, gradually transitioning through red-black to a final vivid red as they mature, so that at any moment the plant displays all three colors simultaneously against the near-black foliage — creating a dramatically ornamental display that performs equally well as a container specimen and in ornamental borders. The fruits are genuinely hot (approximately 10,000–30,000 Scoville) and fully edible — suitable for hot sauces, dried chiles, and as a garnish for dishes where both heat and visual drama are desired. The compact size and outstanding ornamental value make Black Pearl one of the most popular choices for edible ornamental garden design.
sources: All-America Selections — Black Pearl Pepper 2006 Award
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ornamental Pepper Production
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Edible Ornamental Peppers
